questions.question: What is the top speed of a tiger in short bursts?
questions.explanation: Tigers can reach speeds of 49–65 km/h in short bursts. Their hind legs are longer than their front legs, giving them explosive acceleration.

questions.question: Why are tiger stripe patterns important to researchers?
options: Every pattern is unique like a fingerprint
They show which subspecies it belongs to
questions.explanation: Every tiger's stripe pattern is completely unique — like a human fingerprint. Researchers use photographic identification of stripe patterns to track individual tigers.

questions.question: What is a tiger's typical hunting success rate?
questions.explanation: Their hunting success rate is only 10–15% — they fail more than they succeed.

questions.question: How far can a lion's roar be heard?
questions.explanation: A lion's roar can be heard up to 8 kilometres away and is used to communicate territory boundaries to rival prides.

questions.question: How many hours per day do lions typically sleep?
questions.explanation: Lions sleep up to 20 hours per day — more than almost any other mammal. This extreme rest conserves energy in the hot African savanna.

questions.question: What makes lions unique among wild cats?
options: They live in social groups called prides
questions.explanation: Lions are unique among wild cats in living in social groups called prides. A pride typically consists of 2–3 related males, several females, and their cubs.

questions.question: What top speed can lions reach in short bursts?
questions.explanation: Lions can reach 80 km/h in short bursts but cannot sustain this speed. Their power lies in explosive short-distance acceleration.

questions.question: How many species have been recorded as leopard prey?
questions.explanation: Leopards have the most varied diet of any big cat — recorded prey includes over 90 species from beetles and baboons to young giraffes.

questions.question: Why do leopards haul their kills up into trees?
options: To cache kills safe from scavengers
To feed their young more easily
questions.explanation: Leopards are exceptional climbers and routinely haul prey up into trees to cache kills safe from lions, hyenas, and other scavengers.

questions.question: Why are leopards called 'Ghost Cats'?
options: They are extremely secretive and hard to observe
They only hunt during full moons
questions.explanation: Leopards are notoriously difficult to observe even in areas with high populations. Their secretive nocturnal habits and cryptic camouflage earn them the nickname.

questions.question: What is the typical structure of a wolf pack?
options: A breeding pair and their offspring
questions.explanation: Wolf packs are family units typically led by a breeding pair (alpha pair) and their offspring from multiple years.

questions.question: How did wolf reintroduction change Yellowstone?
options: It triggered a trophic cascade restoring the ecosystem
questions.explanation: The reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone in 1995 triggered a trophic cascade that restored vegetation, stabilised riverbanks, and rebalanced the entire ecosystem.

questions.question: Why are wolves considered a 'keystone species'?
options: They build dens that other animals use
questions.explanation: Wolves are a keystone species because their presence controls herbivore populations, which in turn affects vegetation growth and the health of entire ecosystems.

questions.question: How long can a bear hibernate without eating or drinking?
questions.explanation: Bears can hibernate for up to 7 months, during which their heart rate drops dramatically and they survive entirely on stored body fat without eating, drinking, or excreting waste.

questions.question: What makes bears exceptional among large predators?
questions.explanation: Bears demonstrate exceptional intelligence — they can remember food sources from years prior, use tools, and have been observed opening complex locks and latches.

questions.question: How much can a large grizzly bear weigh?
questions.explanation: Grizzly bears can weigh up to 680 kg. Coastal brown bears with access to salmon are the largest, particularly in Alaska's Katmai National Park.

questions.question: How far away can an eagle spot a rabbit?
questions.explanation: Eagles have extraordinary vision — up to 8 times sharper than a human's — capable of spotting a rabbit from over 3 kilometres away.

questions.question: What diving speed can a golden eagle reach?
questions.explanation: Golden eagles can reach speeds exceeding 320 km/h in a hunting stoop (dive), making them one of the fastest animals on earth.

questions.question: What is unique about eagle partnerships?
options: They mate for life and share a nest for decades
questions.explanation: Eagles form lifetime partnerships and return to the same nest year after year, adding material each season — some nests grow to over 2 metres across and weigh nearly a tonne.

questions.question: How do snakes sense their environment without ears?
options: Through ground vibrations and a forked tongue collecting scent particles
questions.explanation: Snakes sense the world through ground vibrations detected by their jawbones and a forked tongue that collects scent particles, analysed by the Jacobson's organ.

questions.question: How can snakes swallow prey larger than their head?
options: They chew it into pieces first
Their jaws unhinge and stretch with elastic ligaments
They dissolve prey with venom before eating
questions.explanation: Snake jawbones are connected by elastic ligaments rather than fused, allowing the mouth to stretch enormously to swallow prey much larger than the snake's own head.

questions.question: Which tiger subspecies is the smallest?
questions.explanation: The Siberian tiger is the largest subspecies, while the Sumatran tiger is the smallest.

questions.question: What is the estimated global wild tiger population?
questions.explanation: The global wild tiger population is estimated at just 3,900–4,500 individuals — a devastating decline from over 100,000 at the start of the 20th century.

questions.question: How far can a leopard leap horizontally?
questions.explanation: Leopards can reach 58 km/h and are extraordinary leapers — jumping up to 6 metres horizontally and 3 metres vertically from a standing position.

questions.question: What is unique about wolf howling?
options: Each wolf has a unique voice and packs harmonise deliberately
They only howl at the moon
It is a sign of distress
questions.explanation: Each wolf has a unique voice — howling is used to communicate territory boundaries, coordinate hunts, and strengthen social bonds. Packs deliberately harmonise to sound larger.

questions.question: What threat do wind turbines pose to eagles?
options: Blade strikes and electrocution from power lines
questions.explanation: Wind turbine blade strikes and electrocution from associated power infrastructure are a growing threat to eagle populations worldwide.

questions.question: Why do snakes shed their skin?
options: Because their skin doesn't grow with them
questions.explanation: Snake skin doesn't grow with the animal. As a snake grows, it periodically sheds the entire outer layer of skin in a single piece, revealing fresh scales beneath.

questions.question: How much meat does an adult tiger need per day?
questions.explanation: An adult tiger needs around 9 kg of meat per day but typically eats only every 3–4 days, consuming up to 40 kg in a single meal.

questions.question: What is the IUCN status of the African lion?
questions.explanation: Lions are classified as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. African lion populations have declined by approximately 43% over the past 21 years.

questions.question: How do bears catch salmon?
options: They stand at waterfalls and catch leaping fish mid-air
They use their tails as lures
questions.explanation: Bears famously stand at waterfalls during salmon runs, catching leaping fish mid-air with remarkable precision — a technique passed from mothers to cubs.
